children.name: 1. Governance & Basics
Objective: Enhance users' confidence in Financial Statements
Auditor must maintain strict independence and neutrality
Board of Directors oversees Management for Shareholders
Risk: Management bias and financial manipulation for bonuses
2. Assertions & Evidence
Key Assertions
Existence: Physical presence of recorded assets
Valuation: Accuracy of recorded amounts
Completeness: Ensuring all transactions are recorded
Rights & Obligations: Legal ownership of assets
Verification Tools
Physical inspection and system data logs
External confirmations (e.g., from Banks/Debtors)
Rule: Third-party evidence is highly reliable
3. Specific Audit Risks (IFRS/IAS)
Revenue (IFRS 15)
Recognize only when performance obligation is satisfied
Advance receipts must be classified as liabilities
Risk: Fictitious sales recorded to inflate revenue
Inventory (IAS 2)
Valued strictly at Lower of Cost or NRV
Risks: Damaged, expired, or obsolete stock
Indicator: High inventory days suggest slow-moving goods
Assets & Impairment (IAS 36)
Impairment needed if Recoverable Value < Carrying Value
Technological shifts cause sudden value drops
Indicator: Continuous negative operating cash flows
Intangibles (IAS 38)
Research Phase: Expensed immediately in P&L
Development Phase: Capitalized only if 5 criteria are met
Auditor verifies technical and commercial feasibility
Foreign Exchange (IAS 21)
Initial recording at the transaction date exchange rate
Receivables/Payables must be revalued at year-end rate
Differences recorded as Exchange Gain/Loss
4. Going Concern Assessment
Responsibilities
Management assesses survival for the next 12 months
Auditor challenges and verifies this assessment
Key Red Flags
Continuous net losses and negative cash flows
Resignation of key operational personnel
Breach of debt covenants or loan defaults
Impact of Failure
Financials prepared on Liquidation/Break-up Basis
All assets reclassified as Current and valued at NRV
5. Industry-Specific Risks
NPOs & Charities
Cash collection methods pose high theft risk
Risk of breaching restricted donor fund conditions
Misclassification due to unskilled accounting staff
Regulated Industries
Strict compliance with health/safety authorities required
Risk: Unrecorded legal penalties and regulatory lawsuits
Service Sector
Accidents lead to legal provisions and claims (IAS 37)
Advance booking models create liquidity risks
1. Core Concepts & Limitations
Objective: Prevent fraud, minimize errors, and protect assets
Auditor's Assessment Levels
Existence: Is the control actually in place?
Design: Is the control logically capable of preventing errors?
Operation: Is the control consistently being followed?
Inherent Limitations
Cost-benefit constraints (controls shouldn't cost more than the risk)
Human error, fatigue, or negligence
Collusion: Staff conspiring to bypass controls
Management override of established policies
Deficiency Reporting: Management Letter (Deficiency, Implication, Recommendation)
2. Sales & Receivables Cycle
Order Processing
Strict credit limit checks before order approval
Pricing must be fetched from pre-approved master lists
Dispatch & Delivery
Goods Dispatch Notes (GDN) must be sequentially pre-numbered
Mandatory customer acknowledgement signatures upon delivery
Invoicing & Segregation
Invoice quantity matched to GDN; Price matched to Sales Order
Segregation of Duties: Sales initiation separated from credit approval
3. Purchases & Payables Cycle
Requisition & Ordering
Reorder levels automatically trigger purchase requests
Procurement restricted to Approved Supplier Lists (ASL)
Mandatory multi-supplier quotations for competitive pricing
Payment Authorization
Strict Three-Way Match (Purchase Order, Receipt Note, Invoice)
System checks to prevent duplicate invoice numbers
Supplier statements reconciled with internal sub-ledgers
Fixed Assets (PPE)
Maintenance of a detailed Fixed Asset Register (FAR) with tags
Maker-Checker principle ensures separate entry and approval
4. Inventory & Warehouse Cycle
Year-End Count Controls
Auditor observes physical counting; management performs it
Counting performed by independent staff (not warehouse keepers)
Use of pre-numbered sheets, written in ink, physically signed
Physical tagging of items to prevent double counting
Cut-off Procedures: Strict halt on inventory movement during counts
Valuation Risk: Identification of obsolete stock (Lower of Cost or NRV)
5. Payroll & HR Cycle
Time & Attendance
Automated biometric systems preferred over manual registers
Overtime requires mandatory supervisor pre-approval
System exception reports generated for abnormal working hours
Employee Lifecycle
Immediate HR-to-Payroll notification upon employee termination
System access revoked instantly to prevent 'Ghost Employees'
Payment: Payroll sheets must be independently reviewed and approved by CFO
6. Cash & Bank Cycle
Cash Controls
Pre-numbered receipt vouchers and daily cash reconciliations
Physical security measures (Vaults/Safes) for cash handling
Petty Cash maintained via Imprest system with unannounced surprise counts
Mandatory IOU slips for temporary employee cash advances
Bank Controls
Prompt ledger recording upon cash handover to the bank
Dual-signature authorization required for bank payments
Independent verification via Bank Confirmation Letters
Regular preparation and review of Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS)
7. IT Controls & Internal Audit
IT Systems
General Controls: Security, password policies, access logs, backups
Application Controls: Specific data entry rules (Range, Format, Limit checks)
Internal Audit (IA)
Objective: Monitors control effectiveness and adds operational value
Independence: Must report directly to the Audit Committee/TCWG
External auditor may rely on IA if objective, competent, and systematic
1. Standard Sequence of an Audit Report
Title & Addressee: 'Independent Auditor’s Report' addressed to Shareholders/Members
Opinion Section: Identifies FS audited and expresses Unmodified or Modified view
Basis for Opinion: Confirms ISA compliance, independence, SAAE, & explains modifications
Material Uncertainty - Going Concern (MURGC): Used if survival risks exist but are properly disclosed
Key Audit Matters (KAM): Mandatory for listed cos; explains 'Why' a matter was significant & 'How' it was audited
Other Information (OI): Confirms reading of Directors' Report for inconsistencies; no audit opinion expressed
Responsibilities of Management: For FS preparation, internal controls, and going concern assessment
Auditor's Responsibilities: To obtain reasonable assurance, acknowledging inherent limitations (sampling/time)
Other Regulatory Requirements: Reporting on proper books, business expenditures, and Zakat deductions
Signature & Date: Firm's name plus Engagement Partner's name for accountability and transparency
2. Types of Audit Opinions
Unmodified (Clean) Opinion: FS give a 'True and Fair' view with no material misstatements
Modified Opinions
Qualified Opinion: Material but NOT pervasive ('Except for' this issue, FS are fine)
Adverse Opinion: Misstatements are material AND pervasive (FS are entirely misleading)
Disclaimer of Opinion: Scope limitation is material AND pervasive (Unable to obtain evidence to form an opinion)
3. Additional Communication Paragraphs
Emphasis of Matter (EOM)
Highlights a fundamental matter already PROPERLY disclosed in FS (e.g., major lawsuit or fire)
Does NOT modify the audit opinion; distinct from Key Audit Matters (KAM)
Other Matter Paragraph (OMP)
Highlights matters relevant to understanding the audit process itself
Examples: Prior year audited by another firm, or Special Purpose Framework usage
4. Materiality & Professional Judgment
Quantitative Materiality
Based on numerical thresholds (e.g., 5% of Profit Before Tax)
100% testing for items above threshold; sampling for items below it
Qualitative Materiality
Small financial errors that change user decisions due to their nature
Examples: Errors that breach bank loan covenants, regulatory licenses, or management bonus targets
5. Subsequent Events (IAS 10)
Types of Events
Adjusting Events: Conditions existed at year-end; requires FS adjustment (e.g., customer bankruptcy)
Non-Adjusting Events: Conditions arose after year-end; requires disclosure only (e.g., natural disasters)
Auditor's Timeline Duties
Active Duty: Must proactively perform procedures up to the Audit Report signing date
After Issuance: If material facts are discovered late, auditor must request FS revision and notify stakeholders
6. Audit Evidence & Representations
Written Representation: Mandatory letter from Management/TCWG taking responsibility for FS
Acts as supporting evidence, but does not substitute the auditor's own evidence gathering
Refusal to provide representations results in a Scope Limitation (often Disclaimer of Opinion)
7. Reporting Frameworks
General Purpose Framework: Serves wide public needs (e.g., IFRS + Companies Act)
Special Purpose Framework: For specific users (e.g., Tax basis); requires restricted distribution via OMP
1. Core Concepts of Misstatements
Error vs. Fraud: Error is an unintentional mistake, while fraud is an intentional act to deceive
Auditor's Objective: Obtain reasonable assurance that FS are free from material misstatements, whether due to fraud or error
Misappropriation of Assets: Physical theft of assets due to weak security or open access
2. The Fraud Triangle
Opportunity: Created by weak, missing, or easily bypassed internal controls
Pressure / Incentive: Financial stress, personal debt, or pressure to meet unrealistic business targets
Rationalization: The individual mentally justifying their fraudulent actions as acceptable
3. High-Risk Fraud Areas
Revenue Recognition
Highest risk area; directly impacts reported profits and market perception
Risk of recording fictitious sales or premature revenue before delivery
Auditor's Response: Strict cut-off testing and direct customer confirmations
Accounting Estimates
Manipulating subjective areas (e.g., bad debt provisions, useful lives) to overstate assets
Auditor's Response: Develop independent expectations and maintain high professional skepticism
4. Key Risk Indicators (Red Flags)
Abrupt, unexplained resignations of senior management or high finance staff turnover
Significant and sudden changes in accounting estimates without logical business reasons
Employees exhibiting a lifestyle that heavily mismatches their known income
Abnormal volume of sales or complex transactions recorded at the very end of the year
5. Management Override of Controls
Risk: Management actively bypassing established internal controls for personal or company gain
Auditor must test unusual journal entries, especially those posted after-hours or on weekends
Investigating recurring large, exact round numbers (e.g., exactly 10 million) in the general ledger
Analyzing narrative descriptions of entries to ensure they match legitimate business reasons
6. Audit Procedures & Detection
Team Brainstorming: Mandatory discussion among the audit team to predict where fraud might occur
Targeted Testing: High-risk and unusual entries require 100% targeted testing, not just random sampling
GL Mapping Analysis: Verifying ledger codes to catch misclassified expenses (e.g., personal travel hidden in admin costs)
Physical Verification: Ensuring sampled assets physically exist to prevent 'paper-only' fraud
7. Governance & Reporting Duties
Strict legal and professional duty to report identified fraud to management and TCWG immediately
Maintaining zero tolerance for deliberate misreporting to ensure a strong control environment
Ensuring exhaustive documentation of all fraud risk assessments and team discussions
1. Definition & Identification
Control & Influence: Entities exercising direct control or significant influence (e.g., Parents, Subsidiaries, Associates)
Key Management Personnel (KMP): Directors, CEOs, CFOs, and decision-makers
Family Ties: Close family members of KMP (spouses, children, dependents)
Other Links: Entities under common control or company-managed employee pension funds
2. Why RPTs Carry High Audit Risk
Complex Structures: Often used to conceal fraudulent financial reporting or manipulate results
Lack of Commercial Substance: Transactions occurring outside normal business practices (e.g., interest-free loans)
Market Value Deviation: Buying or selling assets at prices significantly different from the open market
Hidden Transactions: Deliberate attempts by management to omit or modify transaction records
3. Auditor's Initial Procedures
Information Request: Obtain a list of all related parties, nature of relationships, and year-end balances
Completeness Check: Compare the current year's list with prior years to identify any undisclosed parties
Control Evaluation: Assess management's policies, such as KMP conflict-of-interest declarations
4. Verification & Substantive Testing
Document Inspection: Cross-verify shareholder registers and investment logs with accounting entries
Transaction Tracing: Ensure proper authorization and 'Maker-Checker' segregation exist for RPTs
Expert Involvement: Use independent valuation experts for major asset transfers to confirm fair market pricing
5. Assessing Business Rationale
Substance Over Form: Look beyond the legal structure to understand the true economic reality of the transaction
Management Inquiry: Challenge the underlying business logic for unusual terms or high discounts
Out of Normal Course: Highly scrutinize offline or exceptional transactions for compliance and fraud risks
6. Disclosures & Ethical Duties
Transparency: Ensure all material transactions are fully and properly disclosed in the Financial Statements
Professional Skepticism: Maintain a highly questioning mind; RPTs are a primary vehicle for management override
Independence Preservation: Reject any unethical favors or gifts from KMP that could compromise objectivity
1. Core Concepts & Definitions
Objective: Obtain reasonable assurance that FS as a whole are free from material misstatements.
Definition: Information is material if its omission or misstatement influences users' economic decisions.
Misstatement: The difference between reported FS amounts/disclosures and the required AFRF framework.
2. Types of Materiality
Overall Materiality: A single maximum threshold applied to the financial statements as a whole.
Performance Materiality: Set lower than overall materiality to ensure aggregated small errors do not exceed the main threshold.
Specific Materiality: Applied to highly sensitive areas like Directors' remuneration or related party transactions regardless of size.
3. Determining Materiality (Benchmarks)
Profit Before Tax (PBT): 5% - 10% benchmark, standard for stable, profit-oriented entities.
Total Revenue: 0.5% - 1% benchmark, used for loss-making entities or those with volatile profits.
Total Assets / Equity: 1% - 2% benchmark, used for asset-heavy entities like banks or investment firms.
Normalization: Adjusting the base profit by removing exceptional, one-off transactions before calculating the threshold.
4. Factors Influencing Materiality
Quantitative Factors: The overall size of the entity dictates the monetary threshold; larger entities have higher limits.
Qualitative Factors: The nature of the item makes it material regardless of a small amount, such as regulatory breaches or fraud.
Entity's Circumstances: Loss-making or high-risk companies require stricter (lower) materiality thresholds.
5. Application & Documentation Principles
Professional Judgment: Materiality is not a rigid rule; it relies entirely on the auditor's expertise and firm policy.
Risk Correlation: Higher perceived audit risk requires a lower, more conservative materiality threshold to enforce extensive testing.
Continuous Revision: Materiality must be adjusted downwards if the entity's circumstances, like falling profits, change during the audit.
Required Documentation: The audit file must comprehensively log Overall Materiality, Performance Materiality, and any subsequent revisions.

chapter7.title: Chapter 7: Code of Ethics & Professional Conduct

children.name: 1. Fundamental Principles
Integrity: Being straightforward, honest, and completely truthful in all professional relationships.
Objectivity: Not allowing bias, conflict of interest, or undue influence to override professional judgments.
Professional Competence & Due Care: Maintaining current knowledge and acting diligently in accordance with standards.
Confidentiality: Protecting client information and not disclosing it without proper authority or legal duty.
Professional Behavior: Complying with laws and avoiding any actions that discredit the accounting profession.
2. Ethical Threats & Independence
The 5 Key Threats
Self-Interest Threat: A financial or other interest improperly influencing the auditor's judgment.
Self-Review Threat: Evaluating the results of a previous judgment or service performed by the auditor's own firm.
Advocacy Threat: Promoting a client's position to the point where the auditor's objectivity is compromised.
Familiarity Threat: Becoming too sympathetic to the client's interests due to a long or close relationship.
Intimidation Threat: Being deterred from acting objectively due to actual or perceived pressures from the client.
Independence
Independence of Mind: The state of mind that permits the expression of a conclusion without bias.
Independence in Appearance: Avoiding facts and circumstances that would make a reasonable third party doubt neutrality.
3. Specific Risks & Safeguards
Family & Business Ties: Close family members (spouses, dependents) having financial interests in the client creates severe threats.
Gifts & Hospitality: Must be strictly declined unless the value is clearly trivial and inconsequential.
Long Association: Requires periodic rotation of the engagement partner and senior team members to mitigate familiarity threats.
Fees & Dependence: Undue dependence on one client's fees or contingent fees creates massive self-interest and intimidation threats.
4. Non-Audit Services Constraints
Recruitment: Auditors must never make final management hiring decisions, though they can review candidate qualifications.
Legal Services: Acting as an advocate for the client in dispute resolution severely breaches the advocacy and objectivity rules.
Internal Audit & IT Systems: Designing systems or performing internal audits creates an unacceptable self-review threat.
5. Confidentiality Exceptions & Conflicts
Permitted Disclosures
When disclosure is required by law (e.g., court orders, anti-money laundering reporting).
When there is a professional duty or right to disclose (e.g., quality reviews by regulatory bodies).
When explicit, written consent is granted by the client.
Conflict of Interest Management
Auditing competing companies simultaneously creates direct threats to objectivity and confidentiality.
Safeguards: Notifying both clients, using entirely separate audit teams, and establishing strict 'Chinese Walls' (information barriers).
6. Professional Appointments
Client Acceptance: Must evaluate client integrity and firm competence before accepting an engagement.
Predecessor Communication: The new auditor MUST request client permission to contact the previous auditor to uncover hidden ethical issues.
Second Opinions: Providing opinions on accounting treatments for non-clients requires contacting the existing auditor to get full facts.
7. NOCLAR (Non-Compliance with Laws)
Definition: Acts of omission or commission by the client contrary to prevailing laws (e.g., tax evasion, fraud, environmental breaches).
Auditor's Steps: Obtain understanding -> Discuss with Management/TCWG -> Evaluate their response and FS impact.
Public Interest Override: In severe cases, the auditor must disclose the issue to appropriate authorities, overriding standard confidentiality rules.
1. Appointment & Tenure
First Auditor: Appointed by the Board of Directors (BOD); holds office until the first AGM.
Subsequent Auditor: Appointed by Shareholders at the AGM based on BOD recommendations.
Consent requirement: A valid written consent from the auditor is mandatory before official appointment.
2. Removal & Casual Vacancy
Casual Vacancy: Filled by BOD; if they fail to do so within 30 days, SECP makes the appointment.
Mid-Term Removal: Requires a Special Resolution by shareholders and must be reported to the SECP.
Representation Right: The retiring auditor has a statutory right to submit a written representation to be read at the AGM.
3. Auditor Qualifications (Eligibility)
Public Companies & Large Private: Only a Chartered Accountant (CA) with a valid Certificate of Practice can audit.
Small Private Companies (Capital < 3M): Both Chartered Accountants (CA) and Cost & Management Accountants (ICMA) are eligible.
Firm Responsibility: Appointment is made in the firm's name, but the Engagement Partner takes ultimate responsibility.
4. Strict Disqualifications
Body Corporate: A company cannot be appointed as an auditor; only individuals or firms are eligible.
Past Employment: Anyone who has been a director, officer, or employee of the company in the last 3 years.
Family Ties: If the auditor's spouse or a close family member is a director in the client company.
Criminal Record: Any person convicted of an offense involving fraud cannot be an auditor for 10 years.
5. Shareholding & The 90-Day Rule
General Rule: The auditor, their spouse, or minor children cannot hold shares in the company, its holding, or subsidiary.
The 90-Day Exemption: If shares are held at the time of appointment, they must be disclosed immediately and disposed of within 90 days.
Failure to Comply: Failing to dispose of the shares results in immediate disqualification and vacation of the office.
6. Loans & Business Relationships
Loans: Direct loans from the client are prohibited; loans acquired in the normal course of business (e.g., from a bank) are allowed.
Guarantees: Providing security or guarantees to the client on behalf of a third party is strictly prohibited.
Business Ties: Significant commercial relationships (e.g., major insurance contracts) compromise independence and disqualify the auditor.
7. Statutory Rights & Reporting Duties
Rights: Unrestricted access to books, accounts, branch records, and the right to demand management explanations.
Reporting on FS: Must confirm whether the financial statements present a 'True and Fair View'.
Additional Local Disclosures: Must explicitly report on the proper maintenance of books, Zakat deductions, and valid business expenditures.
1. Objective & Scope
Objective: Obtain reliable audit evidence directly from independent 3rd parties (more reliable than internal evidence)
Balances: Confirming bank loans, trade receivables, and payables
Transactions & Legal: Confirming specific sales/purchases and ongoing legal matters with lawyers
2. The Confirmation Process
Step 1 & 2: Decide what specific information to confirm and select the appropriate confirming party
Step 3 & 4: Design a clear request and ensure the auditor sends it directly to maintain control
Step 5: Proactively follow up if no reply is received within a reasonable timeframe
3. Types of Responses
Agreement: No exceptions noted; internal records match external evidence perfectly
Exception Noted: Discrepancies found; must be investigated for timing differences or actual errors
No Response: Requires sending reminders, followed by mandatory alternative audit procedures
4. Management Restrictions
Validity Check: Evaluate if management has a valid, logical reason for refusing the confirmation request
Fraud Risk: A restriction without valid grounds strongly indicates a potential fraud risk
Reporting Impact: If alternative procedures fail to provide evidence, it causes a Scope Limitation (Qualified or Disclaimer opinion)
5. Confirmation Methodologies
Positive Confirmation
Requires the receiving party to reply in all cases (whether they agree or disagree)
Provides the highest level of reliable audit evidence
Negative Confirmation
Requires a reply ONLY if the receiving party disagrees with the stated balance
Conditions for use: Low misstatement risk, strong internal controls, and a homogeneous population of small balances
Electronic Confirmations
Carries authenticity risks; requires strong controls like digital signatures and encryption
6. Alternative Procedures
Receivables/Sales: Inspecting underlying invoices, shipping agreements, and checking subsequent cash receipts
Conclusion: If sufficient evidence is gained, the assertion is verified; if not, a scope limitation is reported

chapter10.title: Chapter 10: Using the Work of an Auditor's Expert

children.name: 1. Core Concept & Definitions
When to Use: Required when matters fall outside accounting and auditing (e.g., actuarial estimations, property valuations)
Auditor's Expert: Appointed directly by the auditor to obtain independent, unbiased audit evidence
Management's Expert: Appointed by the client to assist in preparing FS; carries a higher risk of bias
2. Pre-Appointment Evaluation
Competence & Capability: Verify professional qualifications, certifications, and industry experience
Objectivity & Independence: Ensure no financial or personal conflict of interest exists with the client
Self-Assessment: The auditor must first determine if the audit team already possesses the required expertise
3. The Formal Agreement
Roles & Responsibilities: Clear division of tasks, ensuring the expert allows review of their working papers
Confidentiality: Strict obligation for the expert to protect sensitive client information
Scope & Timing: Pre-deciding deadlines and communicating materiality thresholds to the expert
4. Evaluating the Expert's Work
Relevance of Methods: Confirming the valuation or calculation models align with accepted industry standards
Reasonableness of Assumptions: Challenging aggressive forecasts and comparing them to historical realities
Reliability of Source Data: Checking if the raw data provided to the expert was accurate and complete
No Blind Reliance: The auditor must maintain professional skepticism and re-calculate samples if necessary
5. Audit Report & Responsibilities
Ultimate Responsibility: The auditor retains sole responsibility for the audit opinion; it cannot be shifted to the expert
General Rule: The expert's name is NOT mentioned in a standard, unmodified audit report
Exceptions for Reference
When explicitly required by prevailing laws or regulations
When necessary to explain the nature of a Modified Opinion
Conditions: Must obtain the expert's written consent and explicitly state that the auditor's responsibility is not reduced
1. Core Control Classifications
Manual Controls: Human-driven actions (e.g., physically stamping an invoice as 'Paid')
Automated Controls: System-driven processes (e.g., system auto-matching POs with Invoices)
Preventive Controls: Proactively block errors before processing (e.g., system rejecting transactions over a set limit)
Detective Controls: Identify errors after processing via automated exception reports for subsequent rectification
2. Application & Input Controls
Input Authorization: Restricting data entry rights (e.g., prices, salaries) to authorized personnel only
Range & Format Checks: System boundaries that reject abnormal values or incorrect data formats
Duplicate Checks: Automated flags that reject previously processed invoice numbers to prevent double payments
Batch Total Controls: Reconciling grouped data totals before and after processing to ensure no entry is missed
3-Way Matching: System mandates a perfect match between PO, GRN, and Invoice before enabling payment
3. Access & Security Controls
Physical Access: Securing server rooms and critical hardware using biometrics, swipe cards, and physical locks
Logical Access: Enforcing unique user IDs, complex passwords, and strict role-based access to specific modules
Segregation of Duties (SoD): Ensuring system developers do not have posting or modification rights in the live environment
4. Processing & Output Controls
Master Data Locks: Automatically locking records during authorized edits to prevent concurrent unauthorized modifications
Exception & Variance Detection: System generates immediate alerts for abnormal trends, missing data, or overdue balances
Output Confidentiality: Restricting sensitive report visibility using password protection and tracking distribution
5. Backup & Disaster Recovery
Continuous Backups: Automated, periodic saving of data stored securely off-site or on isolated servers
Recovery Testing: Routinely testing backup files to verify that data can actually be restored during a real disaster
Service Level Agreements (SLA): Formal contracts with IT vendors dictating minimum system uptime and recovery protocols
6. Modern Audit Technology
Artificial Intelligence (AI): Reduces manual effort by automating ratio calculations and predicting error patterns
Data Analytics: Enables instant analysis of massive datasets (e.g., thousands of loans) to identify anomalies and outliers
Microcomputer Risks: Recognizing that isolated local networks carry higher risks of viruses and downtime compared to centralized systems
1. Internal vs. External Audit
Internal Auditor: Company employee focused on year-round operational efficiency and internal control compliance.
External Auditor: Independent third-party focused on providing reasonable assurance on Financial Statements.
Appointment: IA is appointed by Management/TCWG; External Auditor is appointed by Shareholders.
2. Role & Independence of Internal Audit
Reporting Line: To maintain objectivity, IA must report directly to the Audit Committee rather than operational management.
Systematic Approach: IA follows a structured process of Planning, Testing, and Reporting on multiple business areas.
Audit Committee: An independent sub-committee providing governance and oversight over the IA function.
3. Modes of External Auditor Reliance
Using IA's Work: Relying on previously performed internal audit procedures (e.g., control testing) as audit evidence.
Direct Assistance: Using IA staff to perform specific audit procedures under the direct supervision of the external auditor.
4. Pre-Conditions for Using IA Work
Objectivity: Is the IA department structurally independent and free from conflicting responsibilities (self-review threats)?
Competence: Does the IA staff possess adequate professional qualifications, training, and experience?
Systematic & Disciplined Approach: Is the IA work properly planned, documented, and quality-reviewed?
5. Implementation Rules & Responsibility
Ultimate Responsibility: The external auditor bears 100% responsibility for the audit opinion; it cannot be shared with IA.
Audit Report Reference: The external auditor must NOT refer to the use of IA work in the audit report unless required by law.
Review Requirement: Before relying on evidence, the external auditor must comprehensively review IA's working papers and re-perform sample tests.
1. Purpose & Preconditions
Engagement Letter: A formal written agreement signed before the audit begins to fix accountability and prevent disputes.
Acceptable Framework: The auditor must ensure the financial reporting framework used by the entity is appropriate.
Management's Acknowledgement: Management must formally accept responsibility for FS preparation, internal controls, and providing unrestricted access.
2. Mandatory Contents of the Letter
Objective & Scope: Clearly states the goal is to obtain reasonable assurance, not absolute guarantee.
Division of Responsibilities: Explicitly separates the auditor's duties from management's duties to avoid self-review threats.
Reporting Framework: Identification of the specific framework being applied (e.g., IFRS).
Report Format: Reference to the expected form and content of the final audit report.
3. Optional & Additional Contents
Logistics & Fees: Details regarding billing timelines, fee structures, and management delays.
Involvement of Others: Arrangements for using the work of experts, internal auditors, or communicating with predecessor auditors.
Liability & Access: Clauses limiting auditor liability or defining regulator access to working papers.
4. Scope Limitations Prior to Acceptance
Pre-Engagement Restriction: If management restricts access to records before the audit even begins, it creates a severe scope limitation.
Auditor's Action: The auditor must decline the engagement and withdraw if such limitations are imposed upfront.
5. Recurring Audits (Client Continuance)
General Rule: The engagement letter does not need to be rewritten every year unless circumstances change.
Triggers for Revision: A revised letter is required if there is a change in senior management, ownership, or a misunderstanding of the audit scope.
Regulatory Changes: Any significant changes in legal or reporting frameworks also mandate a revision of terms.
6. Changing Engagement Scope (Audit to Review)
Evaluating the Request: If a client asks to downgrade the engagement from a full audit to a review, the auditor must assess the reason.
Reasonable Request: E.g., A bank requirement changed, and a full audit is no longer legally required.
Unreasonable Request: E.g., Management is trying to hide fraudulent activities or avoiding the provision of specific evidence.
Auditor's Action: If the reason is unjustified, the auditor must reject the request and withdraw from the engagement entirely.
1. Purpose & Core Principles
Primary Objective: Provide evidence of procedures performed, facilitate Quality Control reviews, and ensure team accountability.
The Golden Rule: If an audit procedure is not documented, it is assumed it was never performed.
Self-Explanatory Standard: Documentation must be clear enough for an experienced, independent auditor to understand without further explanation.
2. Structure of Working Papers
Standard Flow: Every working paper must clearly document the Procedure Performed, Evidence Obtained, and the Final Conclusion.
Accountability Logs: Must explicitly state 'Prepared By' and 'Reviewed By' along with respective dates.
Proof of Work: Verbal claims are insufficient; must attach solid evidence like system screenshots, email logs, and signed confirmations.
3. Documenting Critical Areas
Materiality Justification: Must document the rationale behind choosing a specific benchmark (e.g., Profit Before Tax) and the risk-based percentage applied.
Entity Understanding: Maintaining a permanent file with background checks on management, business model, and legal environment.
Significant Judgments: Detailed recalculations of debt covenants, going concern assessments, and related party evaluations.
4. Assembly, Retention & Ownership
Ownership & Confidentiality: The audit firm owns the working papers and cannot share them with third parties or successor auditors without the client's explicit consent.
Assembly Timeline: The final administrative assembly of the audit file must be completed within 60 days of the audit report date.
Retention Period: The firm is legally and professionally required to retain the audit documentation for a minimum of 5 years.
5. Post-Report Modifications
General Rule: Routine changes to the audit file are strictly prohibited after the final assembly period.
Exceptional Circumstances: If a modification is unavoidable (e.g., adding a delayed expert report), it requires explicit Engagement Partner approval.
Documentation of Change: The auditor must record exactly who made the modification, when it was made, and the specific reason for it.
1. Audit vs. Review (Assurance Levels)
Audit Engagement: Provides Reasonable Assurance (High level); results in a Positive Opinion (e.g., 'FS give a true and fair view').
Review Engagement: Provides Limited Assurance (Moderate level); results in a Negative Conclusion (e.g., 'Nothing has come to our attention...').
Work Effort: Audit involves extensive testing (confirmations, physical counts), while Review relies primarily on Inquiries and Analytical Procedures.
2. ISRE Standards Framework
ISRE 2410: Applied when the entity's current statutory auditor performs the review (e.g., half-yearly reviews for listed companies).
ISRE 2400: Applied when a practitioner (who is not the statutory auditor) performs the review for a non-audit client.
Standard Objective: To enable the practitioner to state whether anything material has come to light that causes the FS to be misstated.
3. Acceptance & Rational Purpose
Rational Purpose: The auditor must ensure the client isn't just seeking 'name association' with a big firm for undue credibility.
Red Flags for Rejection: Management integrity issues, lack of independence, unacceptable reporting frameworks, or restricted data access.
Engagement Letter: Must clearly distinguish the review from an audit to eliminate the 'Expectation Gap'.
4. Review Procedures & Risk Model
Analytical Procedures: Using trend analysis and ratio comparisons (Current vs. Prior Year) to identify unusual anomalies.
Tailored Inquiries: Direct questioning of management regarding revenue recognition, related parties, and significant estimates.
Higher Detection Risk: Since detailed substantive testing is not performed, the risk of missing material misstatements is higher than in an audit.
Use of Experts: Practitioners can review (but not re-perform) the work of management experts for complex valuations.
5. Going Concern & Reporting
Going Concern: Reviewers must still evaluate management's assessment of the entity's survival for the next 12 months.
Conclusion vs. Opinion: The review report issues a 'Conclusion' based on limited evidence rather than a full 'Opinion'.
Modifications: Review reports can be qualified if material misstatements or scope limitations (missing tax/property records) are found.
Emphasis of Matter (EOM): Used to draw attention to properly disclosed fundamental matters like special purpose frameworks or going concern doubts.
1. Selection Strategies
100% Selection: Applied for high-risk areas, small populations of large values, or when CAATs are cost-effective.
Specific Items: Targeting high-value, unusual, or critical year-end transactions.
Audit Sampling: Testing a portion of the remaining untested population to draw conclusions.
2. Audit & Detection Risk Model
Sampling Risk: The risk that the auditor's conclusion based on a sample may differ from the conclusion if the entire population were tested.
Non-Sampling Risk: Risk of human error or blind reliance on management even when evidence is present.
Stratification: Dividing a heterogeneous population into homogeneous sub-groups to increase audit efficiency.
3. Error Projection & Evaluation
Projection Formula: Error Rate (Sample Error / Sample Base) × Population Amount.
SUOM: Summary of Uncorrected Misstatements combining Actual and Projected errors.
Decision Rule: If SUOM exceeds overall materiality, a modified opinion is required unless adjustments are made.
4. Nature of Errors & Replacement
Isolated Error: Non-recurring errors that do not require population-wide projection if properly corrected.
Systematic Error: Recurring or system-driven errors requiring full testing and projection.
Replacement Rules: Replace cancelled or irrelevant items to maintain sample size; do NOT replace items with actual errors.
5. Test of Controls (TOC) vs Details (TOD)
TOC (Deviation): Focuses on the rate of control failure compared to the Tolerable Rate of Deviation (TRD).
TOD (Misstatement): Focuses on monetary errors compared to Materiality.
Condition: If expected error (ERD) equals or exceeds tolerance (TRD), reliance on controls is not possible.
6. Sampling Methodologies
Statistical Sampling: Uses random selection and probability theory to evaluate results without human bias.
Non-Statistical Sampling: Relies on auditor judgment; carries a higher risk of human bias.
Common Techniques: Random (software-based), Systematic (interval-based), and Block (sequential) sampling.
7. Factors Affecting Sample Size
Increase Size: Higher expected errors (ERD) or higher desired reliance on controls.
Decrease Size: Higher tolerance (TRD) or use of other effective analytical procedures.
Neutral: The total population size has little to no direct effect on the sample size.
1. Scope & Timeframes
Active Period: Events occurring between the Financial Statement date and the Auditor's Report date.
Passive Period: Facts discovered after the report is signed but before FS issuance.
Post-Issuance Period: Significant facts discovered after the Financial Statements have been made public.
2. Adjusting vs. Non-Adjusting (IAS 10)
Adjusting Events
Definition: Conditions that existed at the reporting date; requires adjustment of FS figures.
Examples: Debtor bankruptcy confirming prior trouble, or inventory sales below cost confirming year-end impairment.
Non-Adjusting Events
Definition: Conditions that arose after the reporting date; requires disclosure only if material.
Examples: Post-year-end factory fires, floods, or debtor bankruptcy due to a post-year-end disaster.
3. Auditor's Active Procedures
Management Inquiries: Questioning TCWG on new commitments, borrowings, or asset destructions.
Meeting Minutes: Reading Board and Audit Committee records to identify significant post-year-end issues.
Interim Review: Analyzing management accounts, budgets, and cash flow forecasts prepared after year-end.
External Checks: Obtaining direct lawyer confirmations and a formal management representation letter.
4. Discovery After Report Signing
Action Plan: Discuss the matter with management and determine if an amendment to the FS is necessary.
Reporting Options
Full Amendment: Issue a new audit report with a new date after performing extended procedures.
Dual Dating: Amending only a specific note; report shows original date plus a restricted date for the amendment.
5. Handling Management Refusal
Before Issuance: Auditor modifies the opinion (Qualified or Adverse) before the report is released.
After Issuance: Notify TCWG and relevant regulators (e.g., SECP/ICAP) to prevent reliance on the report.
Public Action: Take legal steps or use public notices (e.g., statement at AGM) to inform users the report is no longer reliable.
1. Core Concept & Purpose
Formal Confirmation: A written letter from management (MRL) confirming key matters to ensure accountability and prevent future disputes.
Supportive Nature: Acts as documentary evidence that supports other audit procedures; it is NOT a substitute for them.
Accountability: Prevents management from later denying their responsibilities regarding the financial statements.
2. Mandatory Contents (MRL)
Management Responsibilities: Confirmation that FS were prepared according to the reporting framework and internal controls were maintained.
Completeness of Information: Affirmation that all relevant records, data, and access were provided to the auditor.
Specific Assertions: Written confirmation regarding fraud prevention, going concern assessments, and related party disclosures.
Uncorrected Misstatements: Statement that any unadjusted errors are immaterial, both individually and in aggregate.
3. Reliability & Dating Rules
Least Reliable Evidence: While essential, it is considered less reliable than external confirmations or independent documents.
Dating Rule: The representation letter must be dated as near as practicable to, but NOT after, the date of the auditor's report.
Period Coverage: Must cover all periods mentioned in the audit report, reaffirming prior year representations if necessary.
4. Auditor's Response to Contradictions
Inconsistency Check: If management's written claims contradict other audit evidence, the auditor must investigate the discrepancy.
Integrity Re-evaluation: Significant contradictions may lead the auditor to doubt management's overall honesty and the reliability of other representations.
5. Handling Management Refusal
Initial Action: Discuss the refusal with management and re-evaluate their integrity and the potential impact on evidence reliability.
Reporting Impact: If management still refuses to provide written confirmation, it constitutes a serious scope limitation.
Opinion Type: The auditor should issue a 'Disclaimer of Opinion' or a 'Qualified Opinion' based on the pervasiveness of the matter.
Prohibition: The auditor cannot sign the audit report until the required written representations are received.
6. Administrative Requirements
Formal Format: Must be on the company's official letterhead and addressed specifically to the auditor.
Authorized Signatories: Typically signed by the CEO and CFO as they have primary responsibility for the financial statements.

chapter19.title: Chapter 19: Quality Control for an Audit (ISA 220)

children.name: 1. Core Objective & Leadership
Firm's Responsibility: To provide high-quality professional services and ensure audit conclusions strictly follow ISA standards.
Engagement Partner Role: Takes ultimate responsibility for the overall quality of the audit, leading the team and ensuring independence compliance.
Final Sign-off: The Partner must personally review critical judgments and high-risk areas before approving the final conclusions.
2. Client Acceptance & Integrity
Background Investigation: Mandatory checks for fraud or unethical behavior involving client owners or management before acceptance.
Information Sources: Utilizing news reports, global watchlists, and blacklists to assess client integrity.
System Logging: All investigation results must be documented and uploaded to the firm’s internal compliance system.
3. Team Management & Supervision
Direction & Supervision: Senior members must guide junior staff and continuously monitor the progress of audit procedures.
Review Process: A structured hierarchy where every junior's work is reviewed by a more senior member (e.g., Manager reviews Associate's work).
Competence: Ensuring the team possesses relevant industry knowledge and professional skills for the specific engagement.
4. Consultation & Opinion Management
Consultation: Mandatory for complex or contentious matters; involves seeking advice from other partners or external experts.
Documentation of Advice: Both the conclusion of the consultation and the reasons behind it must be formally recorded.
Differences of Opinion: If the team disagrees on a point, it must be resolved according to firm policy; the report cannot be signed until the dispute is settled.
5. Engagement Quality Control Review (EQCR)
Independent Partner Review: A 'Cold Review' performed by a Partner who is NOT part of the engagement team.
Applicability: Mandatory for listed companies and high-risk clients to ensure the team has gathered sufficient appropriate evidence.
Objective: To verify that the engagement team’s conclusions and final audit report are logically sound and justified.
1. Sales & Trade Receivables (Debtors)
Existence: Select 100% of large balances and send 'External Confirmations'. In case of no reply, perform alternative procedures such as inspecting bank statements and GDNs.
Valuation: Review the ageing report and challenge management's provision for doubtful debts for balances outstanding for more than 90 days.
Completeness: Reconcile the debtors' listing total with the General Ledger and Financial Statements, and trace 'Subsequent Receipts' to the records.
Cut-off: Check Goods Dispatch Notes (GDN) near the year-end to ensure sales are recorded in the correct accounting period.
2. Inventory & Stock-taking
Existence (Sheet to Floor): Select a sample from the stock sheet and physically verify the presence and condition of items in the warehouse.
Completeness (Floor to Sheet): Pick random items from the warehouse floor and trace them back to the inventory report (sheet).
Valuation (NRV): Check rates from purchase invoices and ensure inventory is valued at the 'Lower of Cost or NRV'. Verify NRV losses for damaged items.
Rights & Obligations: Send confirmations for third-party inventory and inspect ownership documents such as invoices and agreements.
3. Property, Plant & Equipment (PPE)
Existence & Additions: Select a sample from the Fixed Asset Register (FAR) and physically verify. Check purchase orders and invoices for new assets.
Valuation & Depreciation: Recalculate depreciation expense based on asset life and industry standards.
Impairment: Identify trigger events such as damage or technological changes and challenge management's cash flow projections and discount rates.
Revaluation: Evaluate the competence and independence of the management expert and verify the mathematical accuracy of the revaluation surplus.
4. Cash & Bank Balances
Existence (Bank): Send 'Direct Confirmations' to all banks, including those for loans and guarantees. Trace unpresented or uncleared cheques from the BRS to subsequent bank statements.
Existence (Cash): Observe the physical cash count or verify it through CCTV or video call.
Valuation (FX): Retranslate foreign currency cash at the 31st December rate provided by the State Bank (SBP).
5. Revenue & Long-Term Contracts
Performance Obligations: Allocate the transaction price based on standalone market prices in bundled offers.
Construction Revenue: Verify the '% Completion' by matching total estimated costs and Cost to Date (CTD) with vouchers and payroll. Involve an engineer or expert if necessary.
Occurrence: Take a sample from the sales report and inspect original contracts and delivery notes (GDN/SDN).
6. Liabilities (Loans, Payables & Tax)
Trade Payables: Reconcile supplier statements with the company ledger and check 'Subsequent Payments' to identify unrecorded liabilities.
Borrowings: Verify loan agreement covenants (conditions) and recalculate the interest expense.
Current Tax: Recalculate taxable income by adding back disallowed expenses (provisions/fines) to the Profit Before Tax (PBT) and verify the tax rate.
Deferred Tax: Evaluate the recoverability of the Deferred Tax Asset (DTA) based on future taxable profit forecasts.
7. Other Areas (Legal, Payroll & Leases)

Provisions & Contingencies: Scan legal fee ledgers and board minutes and obtain direct confirmations from lawyers. Verify the accounting treatment (Provision/Disclosure) based on probability (Probable/Possible).
Payroll Expense: Check the 12-month payroll register, recalculate tax deductions, and review contracts to identify potential ghost employees.
Leases (IFRS 16): Recalculate the Present Value (PV) of lease liabilities and ROU assets according to the original agreement and the discount rate (IBOR).
